questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the column vector representation of a vector that moves 3 units left and 5 units up?
questions.hint: Left is negative horizontal; up is positive vertical.
questions.explanation: Left movement means negative \(x\)-component: -3. Up movement means positive \(y\)-component: 5. Thus \(\binom{-3}{5}\). Option A moves right; C moves down; D moves left and down.

source_trace.content_anchor: column vector binom(x,y) where x horizontal, y vertical

explanations_tiered.definition: A column vector \(\binom{x}{y}\) represents horizontal displacement \(x\) (positive right, negative left) and vertical displacement \(y\) (positive up, negative down).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Column vectors use sign to indicate direction: negative for left/down, positive for right/up.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Left = negative number, up = positive number. So (-3, 5) as a column vector.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Movement left: \(x = -3\), movement up: \(y = 5\). Column vector \(\binom{-3}{5}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sign convention aligns with Cartesian coordinates: left is negative x-direction, up is positive y-direction.
reasoning_steps: Write as column vector with horizontal on top, vertical on bottom
misconceptions: Some put vertical component on top or forget sign conventions for left/down.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Left = negative x, right = positive x. Up = positive y, down = negative y.

glossary_entry.definition: A vector written as \(\binom{x}{y}\) where x is horizontal displacement and y is vertical displacement.

flashcard.front: What column vector means '4 right, 2 down'?
flashcard.back: \(\binom{4}{-2}\). Right = positive x, down = negative y.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(\binom{-3}{5}\). Left = negative x, up = positive y.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A vector written as a vertical pair (horizontal, vertical).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that order is always (horizontal, vertical).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: ii) Parallelogram law of vector addition
questions.question: According to the parallelogram law, how is the sum of two co-initial vectors represented?

options.A: By the diagonal of the parallelogram
options.B: By the side of the parallelogram
options.C: By the perimeter of the parallelogram
options.D: By the area of the parallelogram

questions.hint: Think of completing the parallelogram from two vectors from the same point.
questions.explanation: The parallelogram law states that the resultant (sum) of two co-initial vectors is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. Option A correctly identifies this; options B, C, D are incorrect.

source_trace.structural_element: ii) Parallelogram law of vector addition
source_trace.content_anchor: The diagonal of the parallelogram is the sum

explanations_tiered.definition: Parallelogram law: for two co-initial vectors, their sum is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed with them as adjacent sides.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Place two vectors tail-to-tail; complete the parallelogram; the diagonal from the common tail is the resultant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw two arrows from the same point. Complete the rectangle/parallelogram. The diagonal from the start is the sum.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If vectors \(\vec{a}\) and \(\vec{b}\) start at the same point, \(\vec{a}+\vec{b}\) is the diagonal from that point to the opposite vertex of the parallelogram.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Parallelogram law is equivalent to triangle law: \(\vec{a}+\vec{b} = \vec{a} + \vec{b}\). The diagonal vector \(\vec{d} = \vec{a}+\vec{b}\) satisfies \(\vec{d} \cdot \vec{d} = |\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2+2\vec{a}\cdot\vec{b}\).
misconceptions: Some think the sum is the side or perimeter; only the diagonal (from common tail) gives the resultant.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Parallelogram law: sum = diagonal from common tail.

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of two co-initial vectors is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram having them as adjacent sides.

flashcard.front: In parallelogram law, what represents the sum of two vectors?
flashcard.back: The diagonal starting from the common initial point.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does the parallelogram law represent vector sum?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The diagonal from the common starting point of the two vectors.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method of vector addition where the resultant is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a parallelogram to show that the diagonal is the sum, not the sides.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State the parallelogram law of vector addition
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The diagonal from the common tail represents the vector sum.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Addition and subtraction of column vectors
questions.question: If \(\vec{p} = \binom{4}{7}\) and \(\vec{q} = \binom{2}{-3}\), find \(\vec{p} + \vec{q}\).
questions.hint: Add top numbers: 4+2; add bottom numbers: 7+(-3).
questions.explanation: \(\vec{p}+\vec{q} = \binom{4+2}{7+(-3)} = \binom{6}{4}\). Option A incorrectly adds 7+3=10; C incorrectly subtracts x; D subtracts both.

source_trace.structural_element: Addition and subtraction of column vectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Column vector addition is performed by adding corresponding components.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add the top numbers (horizontal) and bottom numbers (vertical) separately.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add tops: 4+2=6. Add bottoms: 7+(-3)=4. Result is (6,4).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector addition in component form: \((x_1,y_1)+(x_2,y_2)=(x_1+x_2, y_1+y_2)\). Here (4,7)+(2,-3)=(6,4).
reasoning_steps: Identify horizontal components of both vectors.
Identify vertical components of both vectors.
Combine results into a column vector.
misconceptions: Some add y incorrectly as 7+3=10, forgetting the negative sign.

glossary_entry.definition: Adding two column vectors by summing corresponding components.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Watch for sign errors when adding negative components.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar
questions.question: If \(\vec{v} = \binom{3}{-4}\), what is \(\frac{1}{2}\vec{v}\)?
questions.explanation: Scalar multiplication: \(\frac{1}{2}\binom{3}{-4} = \binom{3 \times 0.5}{-4 \times 0.5} = \binom{1.5}{-2}\). Option A doubles the vector; C leaves it unchanged; D changes sign of y incorrectly.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar

explanations_tiered.definition: Multiplying a vector by a scalar multiplies each component by that scalar, scaling the vector's magnitude.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A scalar between 0 and 1 shrinks the vector; negative scalars also reverse direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Half of 3 is 1.5; half of -4 is -2. New vector is (1.5, -2).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalar multiplication is linear: \(\frac{1}{2}\vec{v}\) halves magnitude but preserves direction (positive scalar).
reasoning_steps: Multiply each component by the scalar.
Write the result as a column vector.
misconceptions: Some forget to multiply both components, or multiply only one.

glossary_entry.definition: Multiplying each component of a vector by a scalar.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Multiplying a vector by a scalar multiplies each component.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that fractional scalars shorten the vector, preserving direction.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Find the magnitude of the vector \(\vec{a} = \binom{5}{12}\).
questions.explanation: \(|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13\). Option A is \(12-5\); C is \(12+5\); D is product.

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnitude is the square root of the sum of squares of the components.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Pythagorean theorem gives the vector's length from its perpendicular components.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Euclidean norm \(\sqrt{25+144}=13\). This vector is a Pythagorean triple (5,12,13).
misconceptions: Adding before squaring (5+12=17) or subtracting (12-5=7) are common errors.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Length of a vector, computed via Pythagoras.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Mention 5-12-13 as a common Pythagorean triple for quick recognition.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A vector has components \(x = 3\) and \(y = 3\sqrt{3}\). What is its direction angle \(\theta\) from the positive \(x\)-axis?
questions.explanation: \(\tan \theta = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{3} = \sqrt{3}\). \(\tan 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}\), so \(\theta = 60^\circ\). Option A has \(\tan 30^\circ = 1/\sqrt{3}\); B has 1; D has undefined.

explanations_tiered.definition: Direction angle \(\theta\) satisfies \(\tan \theta = y/x\) for a vector \((x,y)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The ratio of vertical to horizontal component gives the tangent of the direction angle.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Divide y by x: \(3\sqrt{3}/3 = \sqrt{3}\). Which angle has tangent \(\sqrt{3}\)? \(60^\circ\).

glossary_entry.definition: Angle from positive x-axis to the vector, found using arctan(y/x).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle from positive x-axis, found via arctan(y/x).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students of exact tangent values for 30°,45°,60°.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A car travels 60 km in 2 hours. What is the average speed of the car?
questions.hint: Speed is distance/time; it has no direction.
questions.explanation: Average speed = \(60 \text{ km} / 2 \text{ h} = 30 \text{ km/h}\). Speed is a scalar quantity (magnitude only). Option B adds direction incorrectly; C and D give incorrect distance.

explanations_tiered.definition: Speed is a scalar quantity that measures how fast an object moves, regardless of direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Speed = distance/time; direction is irrelevant, so it is a scalar.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 60 km ÷ 2 hours = 30 km/h. No north/south needed — just the number. That's a scalar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Average speed = total distance / total time = 60/2 = 30 km/h. Speed has no direction, so it is a scalar.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Speed is the magnitude of velocity. It is a scalar, while velocity is a vector requiring direction.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify distance = 60 km, time = 2 h.
reasoning_steps: Calculate speed by dividing distance by time.
Recognise that speed lacks directional information.
Conclude it is a scalar quantity.
misconceptions: Some add direction to speed (confusing speed with velocity).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Speed = distance/time. Scalar (magnitude only). 60 km/2 h = 30 km/h.

glossary_entry.definition: Scalar quantity: rate of change of distance with time.

flashcard.back: 30 km/h, scalar. Speed has magnitude only.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Scalar: distance travelled per unit time.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with velocity (vector) to reinforce scalar/vector distinction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish scalar speed from vector velocity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Speed = 60/2 = 30 km/h, a scalar. Option B incorrectly adds direction.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Triangle law of addition of vectors
questions.question: If \(\overrightarrow{PQ} = \vec{u}\) and \(\overrightarrow{QR} = \vec{v}\), what is \(\vec{u} + \vec{v}\) using the triangle law?
questions.hint: The resultant connects the tail of the first to the head of the last.
questions.explanation: Triangle law: \(\overrightarrow{PQ} + \overrightarrow{QR} = \overrightarrow{PR}\). Option B is correct; A is opposite of \(\vec{u}\); C is reverse resultant; D is just the first vector.

source_trace.structural_element: Triangle law of addition of vectors

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When vectors are placed head-to-tail, the resultant goes from the first tail to the last head.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Start at P, go to Q (u), then Q to R (v). You end at R. The direct trip from P to R is the sum.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector addition is associative; triangle law is a special case of the parallelogram law.
reasoning_steps: Identify first vector: PQ from P to Q.
Identify second vector: QR from Q to R.
Resultant is from first tail (P) to second head (R): PR.
misconceptions: Some choose QP (reverse) or RP (reverse resultant).

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of two vectors placed head-to-tail is the vector from the first tail to the second head.

flashcard.front: If PQ = u and QR = v, what is u+v?
flashcard.back: PR. Triangle law: PQ + QR = PR.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: PR. The resultant from first tail to second head.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use diagram to show the triangle formed by P→Q→R→P.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply triangle law of vector addition

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions
questions.question: A person walks 9 km east, then 12 km north. What is the straight-line displacement from the start?
questions.explanation: \(d = \sqrt{81 + 144} = \sqrt{225} = 15\) km. Option A is difference; C is sum; D is product.

source_trace.section_anchor: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement magnitude is the straight-line distance from start to finish, found via Pythagoras for perpendicular legs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: East and north are perpendicular, so displacement = \(\sqrt{(\text{east})^2 + (\text{north})^2}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: 9 and 12 are legs of a right triangle. Hypotenuse = 15 (3-4-5 triple times 3).
misconceptions: Adding distances (21 km) instead of vector sum.

glossary_entry.definition: Straight-line distance from initial to final position.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Straight-line distance from start to finish.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 7.5 Applications of Vectors in Two Dimensions

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar
questions.question: Two vectors are parallel if one can be written as a scalar multiple of the other. Which pair must be parallel?
questions.explanation: Parallel vectors have proportional components. For A: \(\binom{4}{8} = 2\binom{2}{4}\). For C: \(\binom{-2}{-4} = -1\binom{2}{4}\). Both are scalar multiples. Option B: ratios differ (2/4 ≠ 4/6).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 7.3.2 Multiplication of a vector by a scalar
source_trace.content_anchor: Two vectors are parallel if b = k a

explanations_tiered.definition: Parallel vectors are scalar multiples of each other: \(\vec{b} = k\vec{a}\) for some scalar \(k\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If ratios of corresponding components are equal, vectors are parallel (same or opposite direction).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Check if one vector is just a scaled version of the other. (4,8) is 2×(2,4). (-2,-4) is -1×(2,4).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Parallelism requires \(x_1/x_2 = y_1/y_2\) (or one component zero). Both A and C satisfy this.
worked_solution: Step 4: Both A and C are parallel to original.
reasoning_steps: For each pair, compute ratio of x-components.
If ratios equal, vectors are parallel.
If both A and C satisfy, choose D.
misconceptions: Some think only same direction counts; opposite direction (negative scalar) is also parallel.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors that are scalar multiples of each other; they have the same or opposite direction.

flashcard.back: (4,8) and (-2,-4). Both are scalar multiples: ×2 and ×(-1).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 7 — Vectors in Two Dimensions
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How to test if vectors are parallel?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Check if components are proportional: (x1/y1) = (x2/y2) or one is scalar multiple.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors that are scalar multiples of each other.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that parallel includes opposite direction (negative scalar).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine if vectors are parallel using scalar multiples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. A: (4,8)=2(2,4); C: (-2,-4)=-1(2,4). Both are parallel. B ratios differ.
